The global demand for cobalt and coltan fuels this destruction, with profits rarely benefiting local people.

Mercury contamination in gold mining areas, from Ghana to Tanzania, poisons waterways and food chains. Artisanal miners use mercury to extract gold, releasing toxic vapors that cause neurological damage. Women and children, who often work in processing, bear the heaviest burden.

These crimes are enabled by weak governance and corruption. Companies bribe officials for permits; governments prioritize revenue over environmental protection. International complicity compounds the issue, as consumers demand cheap electronics and investors seek high returns.

Yet, resistance grows. Communities organize, using African proverbs and indigenous knowledge to demand justice. Legal victories, like lawsuits against Shell, show progress. International pressure, through campaigns and boycotts, can force change.

The path to justice requires stronger laws, corporate accountability, and community empowerment. Africa must assert sovereignty over its resources, ensuring that development benefits people and planet. In exposing these hidden crimes, Mihiingo Kaija calls for a theology of environmental justice—one that honors creation and demands accountability from all.

title: A Carousel of Elite Coalitions: Lamuka, Ensemble pour la République, Union Sacrée, AFC… and now “Sauvons la RDC”
excerpt: The Congolese political class continues its cycle of reshuffled elite coalitions, prioritizing power over purpose. From Lamuka to Ensemble, Union Sacrée to AFC, and now “Sauvons la RDC,” the people wait for meaningful change while familiar faces dominate the political stage.
categories: current issue
tags: lamuka ensemble pour la republique union sacree afc sauvons la rdc congolese politics electoral politics elite coalitions political strategy central africa
author: Sankofa Alkebulan Institute of African Scholarship
content:
The Democratic Republic of Congo’s political landscape resembles a carousel of elite coalitions, where familiar faces reshuffle positions while the people wait for substantive change. From Lamuka to Ensemble pour la République, Union Sacrée to Alliance des Forces du Changement (AFC), and now “Sauvons la RDC,” the cycle continues, prioritizing power over purpose.

Lamuka, formed in 2018, united opposition against President Kabila’s extended rule. It included figures like Moïse Katumbi and Jean-Pierre Bemba, promising democratic change. Yet, internal divisions and strategic miscalculations led to its dissolution.

Ensemble pour la République emerged as a pro-Tshisekedi coalition, supporting the 2019 election. It brought together diverse groups under the president’s banner, but quickly fragmented as alliances shifted.

Union Sacrée, launched in 2020, aimed for national unity and development. It included former rivals, but corruption allegations and policy disagreements caused rifts.

AFC positioned itself as a progressive alternative, focusing on youth and reform. However, it struggled to gain traction against established players.

Now, “Sauvons la RDC” (Save the DRC) emerges, led by figures like Denis Mukwege and Olivier Kamitatu. It promises to rescue the country from crisis, but critics see it as another elite reconfiguration.

This carousel reflects deeper issues: personalization of politics, where coalitions form around individuals rather than ideologies; lack of institutionalization, making parties dependent on leaders; and economic interests, with politics as a means to wealth rather than service.

The Congolese people, weary of this cycle, demand real change. They need coalitions that address poverty, corruption, and development, not just power struggles. True transformation requires inclusive politics, where citizens participate beyond elections.

As the carousel spins, the question remains: when will Congolese politics evolve from elite games to genuine democracy?

title: Behind Beijing’s Rare Earth Blitz and Trade War Escalation
excerpt: China’s rare earth export restrictions are reshaping global trade, technology, and geopolitics. This article explores the industrial, strategic, and African implications of Beijing’s latest moves, highlighting opportunities for Africa to industrialize and secure technological sovereignty.
categories: editors pick
tags: rare earth minerals china trade policy global supply chains technological sovereignty industrial strategy african resources export restrictions electric vehicles renewable energy geopolitics
author: Sankofa Alkebulan Institute of African Scholarship
content:
China’s recent rare earth export restrictions mark a new phase in global trade tensions, with far-reaching implications for technology, geopolitics, and Africa’s development. As Beijing escalates its trade war tactics, the world scrambles to secure these critical minerals, creating opportunities for African nations to assert technological sovereignty.

Rare earth elements are essential for high-tech applications: electric vehicles, wind turbines, smartphones, and military equipment. China controls 60% of global production and 80% of processing capacity. Recent restrictions, including export quotas and quality controls, aim to pressure trading partners and secure domestic supply chains.

The industrial implications are profound. Western manufacturers face supply disruptions, driving up costs for EVs and renewables. Companies like Tesla and Volkswagen rush to diversify suppliers, while countries invest in recycling and alternative technologies.

Geopolitically, this escalates US-China tensions. The US has designated rare earths as critical minerals, investing in domestic production and African partnerships. Europe’s Critical Raw Materials Act aims to reduce dependency on single suppliers.

For Africa, rich in rare earth deposits from Madagascar to Namibia, this presents opportunities. Chinese dominance has historically exploited African resources with poor local benefits. Now, geopolitical shifts allow African countries to negotiate better terms, develop processing capabilities, and build local industries.

Strategic lessons include diversifying partnerships beyond China, investing in human capital for mining and processing, and ensuring environmental and social safeguards. Regional cooperation through the African Minerals Development Centre can strengthen bargaining power.

Africa can leapfrog to technological sovereignty by developing value chains locally. From mining to manufacturing, the continent can create jobs and reduce export dependency. International support for capacity building will be crucial.

Beijing’s rare earth blitz is a wake-up call. As global supply chains restructure, Africa has the chance to industrialize sustainably and assert its place in the technological future.

title: When the Drum Fell Silent: The Most Serious Thing in Africa Today
excerpt: Africa’s deepest crisis is not poverty, disease, or foreign interference; it is the erosion of moral and spiritual conscience. From governance to education, from faith to culture, the continent struggles with a loss of ethical imagination and collective accountability. This article explores the roots of Africa’s moral crisis and calls for a revival of conscience,…
categories: current issue
author: Sankofa Alkebulan Institute of African Scholarship
content:
Africa’s deepest crisis is not poverty, disease, or foreign interference; it is the erosion of moral and spiritual conscience. When the drum falls silent, the heartbeat of the continent falters. From governance to education, from faith to culture, Africa struggles with a loss of ethical imagination and collective accountability. This moral crisis threatens the very soul of the continent.

The roots of this crisis lie in colonialism’s legacy, which disrupted traditional ethical systems. Ubuntu—the philosophy of interconnectedness—was undermined by individualism and materialism. Post-independence leaders, facing economic pressures, often prioritized survival over ethics, creating cycles of corruption.

In governance, the moral crisis manifests as leaders who serve personal interests over public good. Elections become auctions, policies favor elites, and accountability is rare. The people, disillusioned, withdraw from civic participation.

Education has lost its moral compass. Systems emphasize memorization over character development, creating graduates who excel technically but lack ethical grounding. Critical thinking is stifled, leaving youth vulnerable to manipulation.

Faith communities, once bastions of morality, struggle with hypocrisy. Leaders preach prosperity while amassing wealth, eroding trust in spiritual institutions. Cultural practices are commercialized, losing their ethical depth.

The consequences are dire: increased inequality, environmental degradation, and social fragmentation. Yet, this crisis also calls for revival. Ubuntu offers a path forward, emphasizing community, empathy, and ethical leadership.

Revival requires collective action. Education must teach ethics alongside skills. Leaders must model integrity. Communities must reclaim cultural practices that foster conscience. Youth, with their digital activism, can drive change.

When the drum falls silent, it is a call to awaken. Africa’s moral revival will not come from outside forces, but from within—from the rediscovery of conscience that makes the continent truly free.
